sentences.text: My name is Henry Borenstein.
I have a middle name, Hersh, H-E-R-S-H.
Borenstein, B-O-R-E-N-S-T-E-I-N.
The Polish version was B-O-R-E-N-S-Z-T-E-J-N. 'Borenshtain.'
That was the name at birth, right.
No, I don't-- I did not have any.
September 25, 1924.
I'm 70-- 71-- I'll be 71 in September.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland on Leszno Street.
I still remember the name, the number 116 Leszno Street.
L-E-S-Z-N-O Street.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] basically--
OK.
My earliest recollection was, I guess, about four or five years old when my father schlepped me to cheder, and of course, I didn't want to go.
I cried.
And the cheder was only a couple blocks away, just about a block away.
It was on also on Leszno Street, corner of Okopovna, O-K-O-P-O-V-N-A.
And I remember looking at the rabbi.
Well, I remember his name.
His first name's Shaya, Shaya Bord.
He had a beard, and so we called him Shaya Bord.
He had long, big eyebrows.
And I was scared stiff at the time.
And that's about the earliest recollections of my earliest youth.
The next thing I remember, all running-- since I was the youngest, my sister was next to me.
I always used to follow her around.

And she would chase me away because she would go out with her girlfriends, and I was a little boy, but-- And so the next thing I remember is, I guess it was her birthday party, and I was the cute little brother and always admired by her girlfriends.
And the next thing that I remember was going to public school, which is I guess about--
--eight or nine or maybe a little later because I was going to cheder for about, about four years, yeah.
So I went into public school, which was-- I believe it was on Leszno Street.
I'm not sure, but it wasn't too far.
And I stayed in public school until the war, in other words in 1939, September 1, which was the invasion of Poland by the Germans.
OK.
My father's name was Sender, S-E-N-D-E-R, Sender Borenstein.
And my mother's name was Chava, C-H-A-V-A Borenstein nee Minkendorf, M-I-N-K-E-N-D-O-R-F.
And we had family of four brothers and one sister.
Well, there was another sister, but she died as an early-- at an early age, long before I was born.
Her name was Sheindel.
Well, my oldest brother, George, is Rachmiel Zurich-- in English, they took on the name George-- followed by Sheindel, and then was brother Sam.
And I had a brother Jacob, who later perished in the Holocaust, and my sister Faigele, Felice now, and then myself.
My parents had a cafe in Warsaw on Leszno Street.
We were consided above-- about middle, as far as affluence is concerned, you know?
My father was always referred to as the rich Uncle Sender from the family who used to live either in the suburbs of Warsaw in the small shtetls.
And my father was born in Mogelnica, which is just a small town outside of Warsaw.
How do you spell it?
You spell it M-O-G-E-L-N-I-C-A, Mogelnica.
And, and so he had relatives there also.
And whenever they came to Warsaw, they would come to us, and we were the, like I say, the, the richer part of the family.
And--
The restaurant-- Sender's Kawiarnia.
Kawiarnia is the Polish version of cafe.
It's-- we had a regular restaurant, no liquor license, I remember.
The-- we were-- and Leszno, where we lived, was more of a Polish neighborhood.
It was mixed, but primarily Polish.
It was not in the Jewish, quote, 'ghetto area.'
So we depended on the-- on the Christian trade.
And of course, when they got paid, like on a Saturday afternoon-- which, by the way, our restaurant was opened seven, seven days a week.
And they would come in and celebrate a payday.
And of course, send me out to get vodka for them because we did not have a license for liquor.
Oh, you could go and buy vodka?
So I would go by and they would give me a nickel or whatever as the bearer of the vodka.
Regular restaurant food.
Dinners and sandwiches.
And we did have beer.
Mostly was a regular restaurant, full dinners and sandwiches and cakes.
It was kosher, yeah.
And at one-- I understand before my times, they did-- they were closed on Shabbas.
But later on, I guess the requirement was that the big part of the business was on Saturday.
My mother [INAUDIBLE] was very strict.
She did not-- she was very observant.
And she would not come in after Friday-- you know, when she left to do-- when she left on Friday night, that was her time off.
And she would come back, like, Saturday evening after Shabbas was over.
We all participated in the business.
I mean, everybody had to partake and help out.
My brothers would wait tables.
My sister would help out.
And the food was cooked by my mother.
There was all-- it's all family, including myself.
We all had to be part of the business.
This is what our business was.
It was.
I know from the earliest stage, I remember my father used to schlep me to the market early in the morning.
He would go shopping whatever day it was during the week, like 4 o'clock in the morning and would schlep me alongside.
So I would sit down the packages while he was doing the shopping and I would sort of watch for the purchases that he made at the time.
And then of course, he would have a, a person who usually had a wagon.
They would load up the wagon and bring it home and they would put me on top of the wagon and come home.
Orthodox, basically.
As a matter of fact, in the early days, I understand my father was president of a shul, or established a shul.
And then they had a fallout with some of the members, as it usually happens.
And opinions differed.
But that we were primarily Orthodox.
Not really.
No.
I know it was on Leszno 110, which was a few houses away.
It was part of the building complex.
You know, they had a small shul inside the courtyard, in other words, the inside the building.
We didn't close on high holidays.
We did changeover for Passover.
All the-- the the restaurant became kosher for Passover.
I remember my father gave-- were dealt in matzos because we had these big, round matzos that came in round packages.
And we used to stack them up in the-- in the back room.
And so the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no question about being open on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur.
That, that, that was out no matter what the business called for.
And like I say, it was kosher, very strict kosher.
I don't recall.
You mean like a Mashgiach?
No, but we were well-known for being kosher.
So even though we were dealing a lot with the Christian trade-- or Christian customers, but we were kosher.
And I know many-- on many occasions an indigent person would come in and they would feed him and no charge.
That was part of the tradition, you know?
And no poor person went out hungry from our place.
I remember mostly my own neighborhood.
I really don't remember much of-- you know, it's-- when the war came, I was, what, 14, 15 years old?
And but most of the things that I remember is our own neighborhood, which is Leszno Street, [POLISH] around the-- we didn't venture out further away from the house.
And the only thing I remember as far as incidents with my non-- non-Jewish friends was I relied a lot on my brothers to protect me.
And we in our neighborhood had a very strong youth moment.
My brothers belonged to Betar and were self-protected.
Wherever they-- gangs would come in our neighborhood, you know, to beat up on Jews and all that.
They would not succeed.
They were quite well organized.
At one time, I recall-- I was still a youngster-- and a group of Christian guys came in to try to beat up on the Jewish people.
And they organized it so that they let him come into our street.

And they blocked off-- all the houses were built with main entrances in each building so that once you came into the street and each main doors for the buildings were closed, there were no ways of going out other than the beginning of the block or the end of the block.
And this one occasion stands out in my mind.
They, they got together and they blocked off the exit.
And then after they came in and blocked off be the entrance to the street.
Our youth guys.
And I mean, they were pretty strong fellows too.
And once they started attacking us, and they finally came out from all corners and really beat up on them.
And they told them, don't ever come back to this neighborhood.
And it worked.
It was quite effective.

And the especially my brother's-- my older brothers' friends were, like I said, they were part of the Betar and were strictly Zionist minded and that the fact that it was self-defense, as Jabotinsky's influence was pretty strong.
And, you know, you defend yourself and don't rely on anybody else.
I was.
But I was-- in the beginning.
In other words, I was in the beginning stages.
I know my brother went on Hasharah for the summer month.
And, and I went along with him.
Pardon?
It was outside of Warsaw.
I think it was in the town-- I don't know if it was in Zielonka-- Z-I-E-L-O-N-K-A-- in a small town where they-- I remember they-- I went there with them and they were processing film, movie film.
Then they were, like, washing them and re-- recycling it for, for regular pictures or whatever it is.
But that was one of the projects they were doing.
These are things that I remember, but not too well.
But I myself did not get involved in the organizational process.
I remember-- I remember hearing of him.
I myself did not.

As a matter of fact, talking about Jabotinsky, there was a-- my cousins were involved in the-- they organized a march of, of youth or young-- I guess it was from Betar-- to go to Israel, just before the war, in '38, I believe.
There were supposed to be, like, 2,000 young guys fully dressed in uniforms and, you know, from the Betar with the [INAUDIBLE] you know, from the patches and all that.
And they were actually marching-- they were going-- not going-- they started to march-- I think it was called Ripel's, Ripel, R-I-P-E-L, Ripel's brigade or group.
And they were going to go to Israel on foot, and by land, anyway.
And my cousin came in to say goodbye to us.
And I was so impressed in his uniform with the little stiletto on his side.
And, and they said, you're crazy.
You'll never make it.
And he said, well, we're determined to go to Palestine at the time.
And they were marching in force.
Like I said, there was like 2,000 people.
And I [INAUDIBLE]-- they got as far as the Polish border, I think.
They were going to go through Czechoslovakia, Romania.
I don't know exactly where.
But anyway, they got stopped at the border and dis-- disbanded or whatever.
At that time, yes, he did.
But that was--
Pardon?
His name was Pieprz, the last name.
P-I-E-P-R-Z, Pieprz.
And it wasn't Sam.
Sam was the older brother.
This was my mother's side of the family, Pieprz.
And the-- I don't remember which one-- there were four or five brothers.
And one of them was in Ripel's group that went to-- or attempted to go to Palestine at the time.
I don't remember his first name.
Oh yes.
There was a very strong Jewish culture, especially in Warsaw.
My father was a, a lover of Jewish theater.
And, and in the-- in, in, in our restaurant, we had, in the window, posters from the Jewish theater.
So we got free tickets for displaying the posters.
And I remember once my sister and I went to see a Jewish show, [NON-ENGLISH], 'We're Going to America.' It was a comedy.
I still remember scenes from it, the, the traveling.
And the Jewish stage was very strong, and my father [INAUDIBLE] was a friend of Ida Kaminska, which is the-- who was the star of the Jewish theater in Warsaw.
And of course, the Jewish movies were very strong and prevalent and made in Warsaw also.
As a matter of fact, one of my uncles-- my father's brother--
His name was Israel, Israel Bornstein, had a bit part in the movie And I'm still trying to find a copy of this movie.
Somehow, all the Jewish movies have been reproduced in videos except that one, Because I know he was-- had a part in it, like davening before the [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was a tall man with a long beard.
And I would have liked to get a strip of it, a piece of--
Oh, yeah.
Everybody went to see.
But the [INAUDIBLE], he, he was the star of the family.
Nobody else was in the movies.
This was just a bit picture, you know, a bit part.
But it was very interesting.
And the-- well, I could say the theater mostly was in the-- in the so-called Jewish section of Warsaw, which is later became the ghetto.
Oh, I would say a half a dozen blocks in all-- Zamenhof, [POLISH], you know, the-- all the-- which are no longer there, by the way, unfortunately.
You mean as far--
Oh, I remember the war, sure.
But this is-- things start-- when things started changing in Warsaw itself, we were already-- we had left.
But-- we kept--
Do you remember when you heard about the invasion?
Remember-- Oh, that?
I very, very much so.
In September 1, 1939.
Where were you when you heard?
We were in Warsaw.
And as a matter of fact, I got in-- into a little trouble because when the planes came over-- right the first day, there were German planes over Warsaw.
And I ran upstair-- on top of the-- to the roof of the building to watch a dogfight between the couple of planes that Poland had in Warsaw.
And they were really literally having a dogfight, up and down.
And I was up there watching.
And after the alarm was, you know, was over, I came down.
And my father says, where were you?
I says, up on the roof.
Of course, I got a punch.
And, and they were scared.
They didn't know where I was.
And that was my memories.
Later on, when we were in-- the army invad-- I mean, walked into Warsaw, I watched them-- dog-- what do you call it?
The-- Goose step.
The goose step.
The goose steps, right.
And I watched them with fear.
And yet I'm not aware that they're, they're going to be so bad.
And--
Well, it was mixed.
As a matter of fact, my father [INAUDIBLE], he, himself, served in the First World War.
And he remembered the Germans as being good to the Jews.
And he said that when he started hearing stories about the things that they were doing, he says, it couldn't be.
They were such good people, good, friendly to the Jews.
And they couldn't be so bad.
And then of course we started seeing things.
I, myself watched a bunch of Germans cutting off a beard of an old man in the street and having fun and laughing at it.
That was right after they, they came in.
Well, this is just like a flash, back flash.
And then all I remember, they picked out this old man and started cutting the beard with the bayonet, like, you know?
And, and they were just laughing.
That was one of the-- pardon?
They were not too friendly either, unfortunately, because right after when they came in, and they started doing some propaganda by throwing out loaves of bread from a truck to, to the population.
And, and I ran one day, and I was in the bunch.
Some of my own neighbors started yelling, Juden.
This is a Jew.
Don't give him bread.
And this was right after the invasion.
You would think it was--
Very bad, very angry.
And I said, these are the people who we grew up with, and supposedly you made friendly relationship.
Because my father, having been the pillar of the neighborhood, you know, it's a-- he supposedly had friends.
But unfortunately, some of them came to my father and mother and they said, ha, now we'll get you, Jew.
Hitler will take care of you.
And that's when we knew that things were bad.
And but even, even during the invasion when they started bombing the city, we had occasion where the-- when the building started to burn from the incendiary bombs.
My father [INAUDIBLE] said, kindala, we got to save our things.
And--
The building-- well, no, the building where we lived, which is 110, only a couple blocks away.
The restaurant was a low building, a one story building.
But where we lived was a four story house.
And when the houses started burning, my father and my brothers and I, we went down in the basement and dug up a big hole in the basement.
And we took most of our belongings or anything that was of value, including bedding and all the-- and buried everything in that basement.
And, and sure enough, a few days later, the whole building was on fire and it literally collapsed right above that.
And we came a few days after the-- the house was still smoldering.
And we decided to un-- un-- dig the-- dig up the basement to get our belongings out.
And it's a good thing we did because things started smoldering already on top of it.
And that's how we were able to salvage some of the pictures that we had from pre-war.
